nativeRange.habitat: Large rivers and flooded forests; prefers areas with submerged tree roots, overhanging riparian vegetation, and leaf litter; found in both blackwater and clearwater river systems throughout the Amazon and Orinoco basins

decorPreferences: open swimming space in the centre of the tank
large driftwood pieces and roots along the sides and back
robust artificial plants or plastic decor only — live plants will be consumed
dim lighting to mimic tannin-stained Amazonian water
dark substrate (fine sand or fine gravel)
floating decor or overhanging branches to provide shade and surface cover

diet.notes: Dedicated plant-eater in the wild, consuming aquatic vegetation, leaves, fruits, seeds, and algae. In the aquarium, feed predominantly plant-based foods: spirulina flakes and wafers, algae wafers, blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, spinach, lettuce, kale), fresh or frozen peas, and fruit pieces (melon, apple). High-quality vegetable flake and pellets accepted. Offer occasional frozen foods such as brine shrimp or bloodworm as a supplement, but plant matter must form the bulk of the diet. Will systematically destroy any live aquatic plants in the aquarium.

goodWith: oscars and large cichlids (with caution — similar size required)
other large characins (e.g. pacu, silver dollars Metynnis spp.)
avoidWith: live plants — all will be eaten
small fish (may be eaten or outcompeted)
piranha and other Serrasalmidae with predatory tendencies
fin-nipping species (long, conspicuous red fin is a target)
aggressive or highly territorial cichlids that harass peaceful schooling fish

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Myloplus rubripinnis showing distinctive red anal fin and silver body; standard reference photo.
Wikimedia Commons image of Myleus rubripinnis (synonym); clearly shows the red sickle-shaped anal fin. Good alternative candidate.

summary: A large, peaceful Amazonian silver dollar with a striking bright-red sickle-shaped anal fin — the "red hook" — reaching ~30 cm. A dedicated herbivore that will devour any live plants; best kept in groups in a sparsely decorated, large open aquarium with robust tankmates.
careNotes:
The Red Hook Silver Dollar is one of the larger members of the Serrasalmidae family, closely related to piranhas but wholly herbivorous and peaceful in temperament. Adults reach 25–30 cm and are strongly shoaling fish; a minimum group of five individuals is recommended to maintain natural behaviour and reduce skittishness. A tank of at least 450 L with a footprint of 180 cm or longer is necessary to accommodate a school of adults with adequate swimming room.

Water conditions should mimic the soft, slightly acidic Amazonian environments of its native range: pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–15, temperature 24–28°C. The species is tolerant of a moderate range but does not appreciate hard, alkaline water. Blackwater conditions with tannins (achieved via driftwood or Indian almond leaves) are beneficial. Efficient filtration is essential — the bioload from large herbivores is considerable — but flow should not be excessively turbulent.

The most critical husbandry note is the total incompatibility with live plants. Myloplus rubripinnis will consume, uproot, and destroy every aquatic plant in the aquarium regardless of palatability. Tank decoration must rely entirely on artificial plants, driftwood, and robust hardscape. Provide open swimming areas in the centre with shelter along the sides and back of the tank.

The Red Hook Silver Dollar is a strong, capable jumper; a tight-fitting, secure lid is essential — escape incidents are well-documented in the hobby. This species is also easily startled; sudden movements or loud noises near the tank can trigger panicked group bolting, with glass-strike injuries and jumps occurring even in covered tanks if there are gaps.

Distinguish from the common Silver Dollar (Metynnis argenteus, fw-fish-225): both share the laterally compressed, coin-shaped silver body but M. rubripinnis is significantly larger, has the diagnostic bright-red falcate (sickle-shaped) anal fin, and is classified in the genus Myloplus rather than Metynnis. Juveniles can be confused at the point of sale; the red tint on the anal fin is visible even in young specimens.
breedingNotes: Breeding in the home aquarium is rarely reported and considered difficult. In the wild, Myloplus rubripinnis spawns seasonally during flood periods, scattering eggs among vegetation and submerged roots. Captive spawning, if it occurs, requires a very large tank, a well-conditioned male-female pair or small group, slightly raised temperature, soft acidic water, and dimmed lighting. Eggs are adhesive and scattered; parents provide no care and will eat eggs if not removed. Fry are small and require infusoria or finely powdered vegetable-based foods. Successful captive breeding is uncommon and not commercially replicated outside of large-scale aquaculture in source countries.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Myloplus rubripinnis
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters (pH 6.0–7.5, temperature 24–28°C, hardness 2–15 dH), adult size (to 30 cm TL), native range (Amazon and Orinoco basins), biotope, diet (herbivore, plant material, fruits, seeds), tank size requirements, compatibility and behaviour notes. Taxonomy confirmed: family Serrasalmidae, order Characiformes.

additional.name: FishBase — Myloplus rubripinnis
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (30 cm TL), native range (Amazon basin, Orinoco basin, coastal rivers of Guyana), taxonomy (Serrasalmidae, Characiformes), diet (herbivore — plants, fruits, seeds), and temperature range (24–28°C). Confirmed synonym Myleus rubripinnis. FishBase lists lifespan data as not reported; 8–15 years estimated from related Serrasalmidae longevity data.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Silver Dollar Fish Care Guide
additional.notes: Hobbyist trade source covering silver dollar care including Myloplus species. Confirmed peaceful schooling temperament, plant destruction, large tank requirement, and herbivorous diet. Jump risk and cover requirement noted. Tank size recommendation (minimum 75 gallons / ~284 L for smaller species, larger for M. rubripinnis) used to validate 450 L minimum for full adult group.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Silver Dollar species profile
additional.notes: UK trade publication profile covering Myloplus and Metynnis silver dollars. Confirmed Red Hook identification by red sickle-shaped anal fin; noted distinction from Metynnis argenteus (common Silver Dollar — smaller, no red fin); confirmed herbivorous diet and planted-tank incompatibility; highlighted escape risk as high.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Myloplus rubripinnis (Red Hook Myleus) profile
additional.notes: Tropical Fish Hobbyist article on silver dollar Serrasalmidae in aquaria. Confirmed schooling behaviour (groups of 5+), plant incompatibility, large open tank requirement, soft acidic water preference, and peaceful temperament toward similarly-sized fish. Noted that common trade name \"Redhook Myleus\" is used interchangeably with Red Hook Silver Dollar.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity; requires very large tank, soft acidic water, and seasonal conditioning. Egg scatterer with no parental care. Captive spawning events are uncommon and not reliably repeatable in a home aquarium.
